hero.title: The Hardware Revolution Behind AI
hero.subtitle1: From matrix operations to systolic arrays
hero.subtitle1_beginner: AI needs to do an enormous amount of math — regular computers are too slow
hero.subtitle1_expert: Accelerating matrix multiplication with systolic arrays — simulated with cycle-level accuracy
hero.subtitle2: Explore how AI accelerators break through the speed barriers of deep learning.
hero.subtitle2_beginner: Find out why AI needs its own dedicated chips
hero.subtitle2_expert: Explore how weight-stationary systolic arrays work, verified by a cycle-accurate simulator

explosion.title: The Explosive Growth of AI Compute
explosion.title_beginner: AI computation is growing enormously every year
explosion.gapWarning: The gap between demand and performance is widening

matrix.title: The Core of Every AI Operation
matrix.subtitle: This simple operation is all AI needs
matrix.desc: Linear layers, attention mechanisms, LSTM gates — all ultimately reduce to matrix multiplication (GEMM). A single multiply-and-accumulate — one MAC — repeated billions of times.
matrix.desc_beginner: Everything AI does comes down to multiplying and adding numbers — like multiplication tables, but repeated billions of times.
matrix.desc_expert: Matrix multiplication (C=A×W) is the core of all AI computation. A single GPT-3 forward pass requires roughly 350 trillion multiply-add operations.
matrix.billions: This operation repeats billions of times
matrix.formulaDesc: Matrix A(M×K) × W(K×N): requires M×K×N MAC operations

compare.title: Same Operation, Different Speed
compare.subtitle: The same matrix multiplication, executed on three different hardware

cpu.name: CPU
cpu.desc: Serial processing — one at a time

gpu.name: GPU
gpu.desc: General-purpose parallel — fast but with overhead

acc.desc: Dedicated hardware — optimized for matrix operations

compare.revealDesc: Google TPU, Apple Neural Engine, and Samsung NPU all use this principle
compare.revealDesc_beginner: AI chips from Google, Apple, Samsung and other major companies all use this approach
compare.revealDesc_expert: TPU v1–v5p, Apple ANE, Samsung NPU — all built on systolic array architectures for matrix acceleration
compare.cta: Explore it up close in the next HALL

level.title: Choose your perspective
level.subtitle: The depth of explanations will adapt to your selection.

beginner.name: General Audience
beginner.desc: New to AI and curious to learn

intermediate.name: Enthusiast
intermediate.desc: Familiar with basics, want to learn more

sectionA.heading: Why Is AI Computation Slow?
sectionA.subtext: A single LSTM inference requires millions of multiply-add operations
sectionA.subtext_beginner: Regular computers are too slow for AI computation
sectionA.subtext_expert: Weight matrices W_ih(256×8) and W_hh(256×64) computed 24 times — millions of multiply-add operations
sectionA.matrixHint: Even this small 4×4 matrix needs 16 operations...

sectionB.heading: Same Operation, Three Approaches
sectionB.subtext: The same 4×4 matrix multiply, processed three different ways

cpu.name: CPU
cpu.desc: One cell at a time, strictly sequential
cpu.desc_beginner: Computes one thing at a time — the slowest approach
cpu.desc_expert: One arithmetic unit executing sequentially — time grows as the cube of matrix size

gpu.name: GPU
gpu.desc: Rows processed in parallel, but synchronization needed between rows
gpu.desc_beginner: Many workers compute at once, but they need time to coordinate
gpu.desc_expert: Many cores compute in parallel, but row synchronization delays and memory conflicts add overhead

acc.name: Accelerator
acc.desc: Continuous diagonal wave with no gaps — full pipeline
acc.desc_beginner: Like an assembly line — data flows through and gets processed simultaneously
acc.desc_expert: Data propagates in a diagonal wavefront, completing in 2N-1 cycles with 100% utilization at steady state

sectionC.heading: Compare for Yourself
sectionC.subtext: Adjust the parameters and feel the speed difference

sectionD.conclusion: Sacrifice versatility. Focus on one operation.
sectionD.conclusion_beginner: A chip that does just one thing really well is the answer for AI.
sectionD.conclusion_expert: Trading versatility for throughput — a dedicated matrix multiplication chip maximizes compute density.

sectionA.heading: What Happens Inside This Chip
sectionA.subtext: Hundreds of processing elements (PEs) compute simultaneously as data flows like water through the grid
sectionA.subtext_beginner: Hundreds of tiny calculators work at the same time as data flows through like water
sectionA.subtext_expert: An NxN grid of PEs, each performing one multiply-add per cycle in a weight-stationary dataflow

sectionB.heading: One Processing Element: PE
sectionB.subtext: Each PE performs a single MAC operation with three I/O ports.
sectionB.subtext_beginner: Let's look at how a single tiny calculator is built
sectionB.subtext_expert: A single PE contains a multiply-add unit and three I/O ports for input, partial sum, and data forwarding
sectionB.transition: These come together to form a Systolic Array

step1.title: Weight Loading
step1.desc: First, weights are planted into each PE. Once loaded, they are reused for multiple inputs.
step1.desc_beginner: Numbers are pre-loaded and reused many times
step1.desc_expert: Weights are preloaded into PE registers, then reused across batch × sequence length iterations

step2.title: Activation Flow
step2.desc: Input data streams left to right. It is injected sequentially into PEs in each row.
step2.desc_beginner: Input data moves from left to right, one step at a time
step2.desc_expert: Activations propagate systolically — row i is injected with an i-cycle delay for diagonal alignment

step3.title: Psum Accumulation
step3.desc: Partial sums accumulate top to bottom. The final result is produced at the bottom PE.
step3.desc_beginner: Results flow from top to bottom, building up as they go
step3.desc_expert: Partial sums accumulate from top to bottom, with N-1 extra cycles to drain the final results

chip.title: Systolic Array 3D Visualization
chip.subtitle: Weight-stationary dataflow: weights are pinned to PEs, activations stream left→right, and partial sums propagate top→bottom.
chip.subtitle_beginner: Explore this 3D view of a dedicated chip where data flows across a grid in a steady rhythm to perform calculations.
chip.subtitle_expert: NxN PE grid with weight-stationary dataflow: weights are pinned to each PE, activations stream left to right, and partial sums accumulate top to bottom.

simulator.title: Why Do We Need a Simulator?
simulator.subtitle:
Before taping out silicon
A cycle-accurate software model lets us explore the hardware/software design space without fabrication costs.
simulator.subtitle_beginner: A virtual lab to test the chip design on a computer before manufacturing the real thing.
simulator.subtitle_expert:
Cycle-accurate modeling before chip fabrication —
explore array sizes, memory designs, and tiling strategies without manufacturing cost.

sectionA.heading: Predict Performance Without a Chip
sectionA.subtext: Validate designs with a simulator before fabricating real silicon

reason1.title: Real chip = months + millions
reason1.desc_beginner: Building a real chip takes forever and costs a fortune
reason1.desc_expert: 7nm tape-out costs over $50M with 6+ months lead time — and re-spins cost even more

reason2.title: Simulator = instant + free iteration
reason2.desc: Explore the design space freely with code
reason2.desc_beginner: Testing on a computer is free and instant
reason2.desc_expert: A cycle-accurate model lets you freely explore array sizes, memory structures, and tiling strategies

reason3.title: Validate design decisions early
reason3.desc: Optimize array size and memory hierarchy upfront

sectionB.heading: 4-Stage Assembly Line
sectionB.subtext: Raw material (model) passes through stations to become a product (performance results)

sectionC.heading: HW-SW Co-design
sectionC.desc: Validate design with code, improve design through code. This pipeline is the core of accelerator development.
sectionC.desc_beginner: Test the design with code and improve it based on the results
sectionC.desc_expert: Pre-silicon validation and a hardware-software optimization feedback loop

steps.title: Step 1: Compile — Convert Model to ISA
steps.desc: The compiler analyzes LSTM weight matrices, determines tiling strategies, and generates an instruction stream using the 9-opcode ISA.

steps.title: Step 2: Execute — Cycle-Accurate Simulation
steps.desc: The controller dispatches instructions while the systolic array performs per-PE MAC operations cycle by cycle.

steps.title: Step 3: Analyze — Diagnose Performance Bottlenecks
steps.desc: After simulation, a PE utilization heatmap, Roofline model plot, and cycle classification are generated automatically.

execution.title: LSTM Inference on the Accelerator
execution.subtitle: Trace every step of the SolarX weather-prediction model (input=8, hidden=64, seq=24) running on the accelerator.
execution.subtitle_beginner: Watch step by step how a weather prediction AI model runs on the dedicated chip.
execution.subtitle_expert: Traces the full execution of SolarX LSTM (weights 256×8, 256×64, sequence 24) on the accelerator, cycle by cycle.

sectionA.heading: One LSTM Step, Eight Operations
sectionA.subtext: An LSTM cell has four gates (i, f, g, o). Each gate needs a matrix multiply and an activation — eight operations in total, executed in precise order.
sectionA.subtext_beginner: An LSTM is an AI model that filters information through four gates
sectionA.subtext_expert: 4 gates (i,f,g,o): weight×input + weight×previous state, then activation functions — traced at instruction level
sectionA.transition: Let's see how these four gates are computed step by step.

pipeline.sigmoid: Sigmoid Activation — i, f, o gates (ACT_SIGMOID)
pipeline.cell: Cell State Update: c_t = f⊙c_'{'t-1'}' + i⊙g

highLevel.step1: 📥 Load Data — Load input features (temperature, wind speed, etc.) and weights into the accelerator's on-chip memory.
highLevel.step1_beginner: 📥 Prepare Data — Load weather info (temperature, wind, etc.) and the AI's learned knowledge onto the chip.
highLevel.step1_expert: 📥 Weight & input loading — W_ih(256×8) and W_hh(256×64) preloaded to SRAM. Input fetched from DRAM each timestep
highLevel.step2: ✖️ Matrix Multiply — Perform W×input matrix multiplication for each of the 4 gates (i, f, g, o). This is the core step, taking ~85% of total cycles.
highLevel.step2_beginner: ✖️ Core Computation — Apply the AI's learned patterns to the input data. This is the most important step, taking 85% of total time.
highLevel.step2_expert: ✖️ Matrix multiply — W_ih×input (1 tile) + W_hh×previous state (8 tiles). Takes ~85% of all cycles. Weight tile reuse is the key optimization.
highLevel.step3: 🔀 Activate & Combine — Apply sigmoid (i, f, o) and tanh (g) activations, then combine gate values to update cell memory and hidden state.
highLevel.step3_beginner: 🔀 Combine Results — Transform results with special functions and decide what to remember and what to forget.
highLevel.step3_expert: 🔀 Sigmoid (i,f,o) and tanh (g) activation → element-wise operations to update cell state and hidden state
highLevel.step4: 🔁 Repeat — Repeat the above process for all 24 timesteps to produce the final weather prediction.
highLevel.step4_beginner: 🔁 Repeat — Repeat the above 24 times to complete a full day's weather forecast.
highLevel.step4_expert: 🔁 Repeat for 24 timesteps — each output feeds into the next step. Weights stay in PE registers and are reused.

lab.title: Performance Lab
lab.subtitle: Adjust parameters and analyze design trade-offs in real time using the Roofline model, PE heatmap, and cycle breakdown charts.
lab.subtitle_beginner: Move the sliders and watch the charts change in real time!
lab.subtitle_expert: Adjust array size, batch size, and sequence length to observe Roofline shifts, PE utilization patterns, and cycle breakdowns in real time.

metricPreview.hint: Adjust parameters below and observe how these metrics change

guide.title: Try these experiments:
guide.tip1: Change Array Size from 4→16. How does utilization change?
guide.tip2: Increase Batch Size. What happens to total cycles?

lab.beginnerGuide: Move the sliders to change values. The charts will update in real time!

annotations.roofline: If the point is below the diagonal (memory ceiling), memory is the bottleneck. Below the horizontal line (compute ceiling), compute is the bottleneck.
annotations.roofline_beginner: The higher the blue dot, the better the performance. Above the diagonal means compute — not memory — is the limit.
annotations.roofline_expert: The ridge point = peak compute ÷ bandwidth. Left of it means memory is the bottleneck; right means compute is. The workload's arithmetic intensity determines which side you land on.
annotations.heatmap: Bright cells mean that PE is busy computing. Dark cells are idle. More uniformly bright is better.
annotations.heatmap_beginner: Bright = working, Dark = resting. All bright means efficient!
annotations.heatmap_expert: Utilization = active cycles ÷ total cycles per PE. Diagonal data skew causes edge PEs to be underutilized. The ratio of tile size to array size is the key factor.
annotations.breakdown: Shows how total time is spent across compute, memory access, activation functions, and stalls.
annotations.breakdown_beginner: Blue (compute) is good, Red (waiting) is wasted time.
annotations.breakdown_expert: Stalls come from pipeline bubbles and DRAM access delays. A higher compute ratio means better efficiency. If memory-bound, try increasing SRAM size or changing the tiling strategy.

rawMetrics.title: Raw Metrics
rawMetrics.desc: Raw performance metrics from the simulator output.

architecture.title: Full System Architecture
architecture.subtitle:
From compiler to silicon
See how every layer of AccSim interacts.
architecture.subtitle_beginner:
A simple tour of AccSim's building blocks.
See what each component does.
architecture.subtitle_expert:
Full data flow, control flow, and future C++ replacement boundaries.
Explore AccSim's architecture in detail.

compiler.name: Compiler
compiler.desc: Extracts weights from PyTorch/NumPy models, determines tiling strategies, and emits an instruction stream in the 9-opcode ISA. lstm_compiler.py provides an LSTM-specific compilation pass.
compiler.desc_beginner: Translates the AI model into a language the chip can understand.
compiler.desc_expert: Model weights → Tiler (matrix partitioning) → Scheduler (execution ordering) → instruction stream. Includes an LSTM-specific compilation pass.

controller.name: Controller
controller.desc: Receives the instruction stream and dispatches operations to each execution unit. The _read_from_any_sram() method transparently reads from whichever of the three SRAM buffers holds the required data.
controller.desc_beginner: The conductor that delivers instructions to each component.
controller.desc_expert: Instruction dispatch loop with automatic 3-buffer SRAM lookup and per-opcode routing to execution units.

systolicArray.name: Systolic Array
systolicArray.desc: NxN weight-stationary array. Each PE performs a MAC operation every cycle. Activations propagate left→right; partial sums flow top→bottom. Operates with cycle-accurate fidelity.
systolicArray.desc_beginner: Hundreds of calculators working in rows simultaneously — the core computation unit.
systolicArray.desc_expert: NxN PE grid with weight-stationary dataflow and cycle-accurate execution. Diagonal wavefront with 2N-1 cycle latency.

sram.name: SRAM (3-Buffer)
sram.desc: A dedicated triple-buffer structure for input activations, weights, and output/partial sums. Maximizes on-chip bandwidth and minimizes costly DRAM accesses.
sram.desc_beginner: Three fast storage areas that keep frequently used data close by for speed.
sram.desc_expert: Triple buffer for activations, weights, and outputs. Maximizes on-chip bandwidth with double-buffering for overlapped compute and memory access.

dram.name: DRAM
dram.desc: Off-chip memory. An integrated latency model simulates cycle-level delays incurred on SRAM misses, enabling accurate memory-bound performance analysis.
dram.desc_beginner: A large but slow storage area. All data lives here and is fetched to fast storage when needed.
dram.desc_expert: Off-chip memory with a latency model — when data isn't in SRAM, a cycle penalty is incurred. Bandwidth is parameterized in bytes per cycle.

analysis.name: Analysis Module
analysis.desc: Collects simulation results and auto-generates Roofline plots, PE heatmaps, cycle breakdowns, and HW/SW numerical comparison reports.
analysis.desc_beginner: Turns results into easy-to-read graphs and charts.
analysis.desc_expert: Collects metrics and auto-generates Roofline plots, PE utilization heatmaps, cycle breakdowns, and hardware vs software comparisons.

architecture.clickHint: Click a component to view details

demo.title: SolarX Mission Control
demo.subtitle:
Watch the SolarX solar power prediction LSTM perform real inference on the AccSim accelerator.
Witness the real-time race between CPU and accelerator.
demo.subtitle_beginner: See how fast a dedicated AI chip can predict solar power generation compared to a regular computer.
demo.subtitle_expert:
Compare SolarX LSTM inference (weights 256×8, 256×64)
between CPU sequential execution and AccSim accelerator at cycle-level detail.

scenario.title: Scenario
scenario.desc: Predict 24-hour solar power generation using LSTM. 8 weather features (temperature, humidity, wind speed, irradiance, etc.) are input to produce hourly power output predictions.
scenario.desc_beginner: An AI that looks at weather data and predicts how much solar power will be generated tomorrow.
scenario.desc_expert: LSTM model: 8 inputs, 64 hidden units, sequence length 24. About 786K multiply-adds total, dominated by the W_hh computation (8 weight tiles).

insights.title: Insights
insights.latencyDesc: The AccSim accelerator completes a 24-hour forecast in {cycles} cycles ({time}). Fast enough for real-time decision making.
insights.latencyDesc_beginner: The dedicated chip finishes a full day's prediction in the blink of an eye ({time}).
insights.latencyDesc_expert: Total {cycles} cycles at 1GHz = {time}. Most of the latency comes from W_hh's 8 weight tiles × 24 timestep matrix multiplications.
insights.efficiencyDesc: Dedicated hardware uses {ratio}× less energy than CPU for the same computation.
insights.tradeoffDesc: FP32 matmul error < 1e-10. Quantization (INT8) increases speed but requires careful accuracy-performance trade-off analysis.

conclusion.title: Why All of This Matters
conclusion.desc:
Building a simulator, understanding the architecture, analyzing performance — all to verify that a real AI model runs efficiently on dedicated hardware.
SolarX LSTM inference can be performed in real-time on the AccSim accelerator.
conclusion.desc_beginner:
We've confirmed that a dedicated chip can run AI fast and efficiently.
This is why AI-specific chips are needed.
conclusion.desc_expert:
Cycle-level verification confirms that real-time inference is achievable.
Roofline analysis reveals where memory-bound optimizations can further improve performance.

Learn about the motivation, implementation, and technology behind it.

A project that shows how AI-dedicated chips work.
Learn about the motivation and technology used.

about.behindScenesDesc: The technologies behind AccSim and why each was chosen.
about.closingMessage: Thank you for visiting the exhibition.

motivation.title: Motivation
motivation.desc:
While training an LSTM model for the SolarX solar power forecasting project, the question arose: "What if this model ran on a dedicated AI chip?"
The goal was to build a cycle-accurate simulator that enables exploration of both hardware design and software optimization — without an actual ASIC tape-out.

techStack.title: Technology Stack
techStack.simulator: Simulator Engine: Python 3.11+, NumPy (np.float64 high-precision arithmetic), pytest
techStack.frontend: Frontend: Next.js 15, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Three.js (3D visualization), Recharts
techStack.backend: Backend API: Python FastAPI, direct AccSim engine integration, WebSocket real-time streaming

why.title: Why Build This
why.desc: To develop an integrated understanding of hardware architecture and software compilation by implementing an AI accelerator from scratch and running a real neural network model (SolarX LSTM) on top of it.
why.desc_beginner: I wanted to build an AI chip from scratch and see how it works.
why.desc_expert: Built a systolic array, 9-opcode ISA, and compiler pipeline from scratch to develop an integrated understanding of hardware-software co-design.

how.title: How It Was Built
how.desc: A weight-stationary systolic array was modeled at PE granularity with cycle accuracy. A 9-opcode ISA and compiler pipeline (tiling → scheduling → code generation) were designed from scratch, with clear module boundaries allowing a future C++ replacement.
how.desc_beginner: Built a virtual chip in code and tested it by actually running an AI model.
how.desc_expert: PE-level cycle-accurate modeling with a 9-opcode ISA and tiling/scheduling pipeline. Core modules are designed for future C++ replacement.

results.title: What Was Achieved
results.desc: Verified LSTM inference accuracy with matmul error < 1e-10 and NumPy vs PyTorch deviation < 1e-5. Roofline analysis confirmed W_hh operations are memory-bound, and tile size optimization improved PE utilization.
results.desc_beginner: The simulator produced nearly identical results to reality, and we found ways to improve performance.
results.desc_expert: Matrix multiply error < 1e-10, LSTM verification error < 1e-5. Roofline analysis confirmed W_hh as memory-bound, and tile optimization improved PE utilization.

aboutMe.name: Myeonwoo Lee
aboutMe.school: Incheon National University, Dept. of Computer Science
aboutMe.bio: A computer science undergraduate interested in AI accelerator architecture and system software. My goal is to design efficient AI inference systems through HW-SW co-design.
